repo_notes.content: RLix is a Ray-based control plane for concurrent LLM reinforcement learning on shared GPUs. It lets multiple RL jobs share GPU capacity so researchers can run more experiments and wait less. ROLL provides the training and inference workers; RLix manages GPU allocation and pipeline lifecycle. Design principles: fail fast, no fault tolerance, no backward compatibility, happy-path only.

pages.title: Overview
pages.purpose: Introduce RLix, explain its purpose as a Ray-based control plane for concurrent LLM RL training with GPU time-sharing, and provide a high-level architectural summary

page_notes.content: RLix is control-plane only; ROLL provides the workers. The core insight: RL pipelines alternate training and rollout, leaving GPUs idle. RLix fills those gaps by running multiple pipelines concurrently with two-level sharing: cross-pipeline elastic resize and within-pipeline multi-LoRA. Tagline: 'Run more RL experiments. Wait less for GPUs.'

pages.title: Architecture at a Glance
pages.purpose: Present the high-level system architecture showing Control Plane, Pipelines, GPU Clusters, and their relationships

page_notes.content: Three singleton Ray actors (orchestrator, scheduler, resource_manager) plus one coordinator actor per pipeline. The scheduler is decomposed into three focused modules: core scheduling logic, a gap-ratio planner for generation allocation, and a tracer for Perfetto GPU timelines.

pages.title: Key Concepts
pages.purpose: Define fundamental terminology: pipelines, coordinators, GPU time-sharing, priority-based scheduling, LoRA adapters, rollout workers, and scheduling stages

page_notes.content: Priority enum has 7 tiers; only GENERATION is preemptable. Cluster types: actor_train, actor_infer (generation), critic, reference, reward (CPU-only). Cluster name constants and all protocol types are consolidated in the protocol types module. Only actor_infer clusters are elastically resized.

pages.title: Getting Started
pages.purpose: Guide users through initial setup, running their first pipeline, and understanding basic configuration

page_notes.content: See README.md for a quick-start code snippet showing the six-step workflow: init RLix, allocate pipeline ID, register GPU topology, admit pipeline, create coordinator, create and run pipeline actor.

pages.title: Installation and Setup
pages.purpose: Cover dependencies, environment setup, and initial configuration

page_notes.content: setup_env.sh creates the rlix conda environment with Python 3.10, CUDA 12.4, and installs ROLL and RLix. Environment variable RLIX_REQUIRED_GPUS_PER_NODE enforces strict GPU-per-node topology at init.

pages.title: Quick Start Example
pages.purpose: Walk through running the example script, explain the initialization sequence, and show expected output

page_notes.content: The example script supports single pipeline, two full-finetune pipelines, or full-finetune plus multi-LoRA. Pipeline class is resolved from the YAML config via pipeline_cls.

pages.title: Configuration Basics
pages.purpose: Introduce YAML configuration files, explain cluster_tp_configs and cluster_device_mappings, and show basic pipeline config structure

page_notes.content: Required config keys: cluster_tp_configs and cluster_device_mappings. Multi-LoRA requires megatron_train strategy and is_lora_optimizer_isolated=true. train_device_mapping must be a subset of infer_device_mapping.

pages.title: Control Plane
pages.purpose: Document the singleton control plane components (Orchestrator, Scheduler, ResourceManager) that manage all pipelines

page_notes.content: Three singleton actors created by rlix.init(create_if_missing=True). The init entry point aliases through the client module. Actor names and protocol types (Priority, ProgressReport) are defined in the protocol types module.

pages.title: Orchestrator
pages.purpose: Explain Orchestrator's role in pipeline lifecycle management: allocate, register, admit, kill, shutdown

page_notes.content: Owns the four-step pipeline lifecycle: allocate_pipeline_id, register_pipeline (validates topology via the validation module), admit_pipeline (creates coordinator actor), kill_pipeline (tears down coordinator and releases scheduler state). Uses env-var-configurable timeouts.

pages.title: Scheduler
pages.purpose: Document SchedulerImpl's priority-based GPU allocation, request/release APIs, and central scheduling loop

page_notes.content: SchedulerImpl is the core scheduling actor. It is decomposed into three modules: the scheduler itself (lifecycle, request management, plan execution), a planner module (stateless gap-ratio generation planning), and a tracer module (Perfetto GPU timeline tracing via composition). Allocation ops use explicit rank-to-GPU dicts. Core APIs: request_gpus, notify_ready_to_release, report_progress.

pages.title: Scheduler Tracer
pages.purpose: Document the SchedulerTracer component that records GPU timeline events for Perfetto visualization

page_notes.content: SchedulerTracer is a separate module composed into SchedulerImpl. It wraps tg4perfetto integration for recording GPU allocation, queue wait, and scheduling events as Perfetto trace slices. Conditionally enabled when tg4perfetto is installed.

pages.title: Resource Manager
pages.purpose: Explain ResourceManager's role in GPU topology validation and cluster resource snapshots

page_notes.content: Validates GPU topology at startup and provides cluster snapshots. init_topology enforces consistent gpus-per-node across all alive Ray nodes.

pages.title: Pipeline Coordination
pages.purpose: Document the PipelineCoordinator actor's role in managing individual pipelines and coordinating with the control plane

page_notes.content: The coordinator calls request_gpus and notify_ready_to_release on behalf of the pipeline, keeping ROLL training code fully decoupled from GPU scheduling (recipe-transparent scheduling). Caches actor handles for performance. resize_infer and sync_lora_weights are serialized via a lock with configurable timeout.

pages.title: Pipeline Actor Creation
pages.purpose: Detail how PipelineCoordinator determines pipeline type, injects environment variables, and creates the pipeline actor

page_notes.content: Pipeline class is resolved from the YAML config pipeline_cls field. sleep_level=2 and offload_nccl=True are auto-defaulted when unset. Reward cluster must be CPU-only. Injects pipeline identity and thread-limiting env vars into ROLL runtime_env.

pages.title: Pipeline Implementations
pages.purpose: Document the two pipeline implementations: RollFullFinetunePipeline for full finetuning and RollMultiLoraPipeline for multi-LoRA training

page_notes.content: Full-finetune wraps ROLL's agentic pipeline with elastic GPU expand/shrink. Multi-LoRA trains multiple adapters on a shared base model with isolated optimizers per adapter. Both follow the same seven-phase training loop structure.

pages.title: Training Loop Phases
pages.purpose: Detail the sequential phases in the training loop: expand, rollout, shrink, old log probs, advantage, training, sync

page_notes.content: Seven Priority tiers map to phases. GENERATION (priority 6) is the only preemptable phase. For multi-LoRA correctness: adapter must be disabled during REF_LOG_PROBS and enabled during OLD_LOG_PROBS. No GAE critic in multi-LoRA mode.

pages.title: GPU Resource Management
pages.purpose: Explain how RLix achieves GPU time-sharing through priority-based scheduling, elastic workers, and background rebalancing

page_notes.content: The scheduler rebalances continuously using ProgressReport demand signals. Two-level sharing: cross-pipeline via elastic resize of rollout workers, within-pipeline multi-LoRA via adapter weight sync. Gap-ratio generation planning is handled by a dedicated planner module.

pages.title: Gap-Ratio Planner
pages.purpose: Document the gap-ratio algorithm that distributes generation GPU capacity proportionally across pipelines

page_notes.content: The planner is a set of stateless module functions extracted from the scheduler. It snapshots current DP worker allocation per pipeline, then distributes available GPU capacity proportionally to each pipeline's remaining rollout demand. Produces execution plans consumed by the scheduler's plan-apply loop.

pages.title: Scheduling Priorities and Allocation
pages.purpose: Document the seven priority levels (0-6), which stages are preemptable, and how priority determines GPU allocation strategy

page_notes.content: Non-GENERATION priorities get fixed device_mapping GPUs and preempt generation workers when necessary. GENERATION uses the gap-ratio planner to distribute capacity proportionally to trajectory demand. ExecutionPlan encodes the resolved allocation and shrink ops.

pages.title: Elastic Rollout Workers
pages.purpose: Explain how actor_infer workers expand onto idle GPUs and shrink when higher-priority stages need capacity

page_notes.content: Expand adds DP ranks via ROLL resize_infer. Shrink is two-phase: stop routing, then sleep_level=2 drops model weights from VRAM. Inference weights are loaded only while a worker is active and released when the worker shrinks.

pages.title: Model Synchronization
pages.purpose: Document the weight sync mechanism for pushing trained weights to inference workers

page_notes.content: After each training tick, ROLL builds a versioned CPU bucket cache and replays it to vLLM workers via IPC (colocated) or NCCL broadcast (remote). The model update service handles timeout parsing and lazy port allocation. Triggered from the coordinator.
